note:
位于奥斯陆以西40英里的小镇Hønefoss郊区的一个5000平方米的仓库，是挪威最大的比特币采矿公司的一个场地，后面有一排装满碎木头的蓝色大箕斗。
热空气正通过从仓库里卷出来的弯曲波纹管被泵入12个箕斗。尽管下雪了，但这些原木需要几天时间才能晾干，之后当地的伐木工人会感激免费的服务，把它们带走出售。
在仓库内工作的数千台堆叠的高计算机服务器（称为矿工）释放出的一些所谓的废热正在加热木材。这是挪威Kryptovault公司拥有的两个这样的网站之一。该公司预计，今年晚些时候，其采矿业将占全球比特币网络计算和处理能力的略低于1%。
比特币挖掘是一种通过解决复杂的计算数学难题并在此过程中验证交易来获取加密货币的过程，以能源密集著称。剑桥大学比特币电力消费指数（bitcoin Electric consumption index）的最新计算结果表明，该行业一年消耗的能源超过了包括阿根廷、巴基斯坦和波兰在内的许多国家。
热是不可避免的废物副产品。尽管通风机发出的噪音如此之大，以至于在邻居投诉后，该公司不得不花费约150万英镑用于隔热，但赫内福斯仓库的高温区域可能达到55摄氏度。
对许多人来说，在能源价格飙升之际，这可能是一个企业不可持续性的进一步证据。波士顿大学全球发展政策中心高级研究员罗伯特·麦考利（Robert McCauley）最近将这个企业描述为“比麦道夫式庞氏骗局更糟糕”。
批评者们肯定在盘旋。俄罗斯央行已提议取缔该国的所有加密货币业务，而中国已经这样做了。瑞典监管机构呼吁欧洲采取类似措施。挪威地区发展部长比约恩·阿里尔德·格拉姆告诉《卫报》，挪威政府正在审查其选择。
格拉姆说：“虽然从长远来看，加密采矿及其基础技术可能会带来一些好处，但现在很难证明广泛使用可再生能源是合理的。”。“地方政府和地区发展部目前正在审查潜在的政策措施，以应对加密采矿造成的大量能源使用带来的挑战。”
但该行业正在酝酿反击，仅使用可再生能源的Kryptovault就是其中的一部分。本周，亿万富翁迈克尔·诺沃格拉茨（Michael Novogratz）发起了一项关于能源使用和社会责任的可持续性计划，称该行业需要解决“围绕其对环境有害的错误说法”。诺沃格拉茨是一家寻求成为“加密技术的高盛”（Goldman Sachs of crypto）的数字资产公司。
一些批评人士将其描述为“绿色清洗”，但在一个迄今为止对其活动缺乏透明度、更喜欢保密而不是同情的世界里，这是一种转变。
39岁的Kjetil Hove Pettersen是Kryptovault的首席执行官，他在决定将一种爱好转变为一项业务后与朋友一起创立了该公司。他说，他还认为该行业是时候回击流行的说法了。
他说：“如果你看看全球任何特定事物的总能源成本，它都将是巨大的——我认为我们总能与一个欧洲小国相比。”。“这还包括传统的金矿开采，其能耗是比特币开采的四倍多。”
比特币的倡导者认为，比特币提供了与他人即时交换价值的机会，无需使用银行等第三方，无需获得许可，而且几乎是免费的。他们表示，尽管国际货币基金组织（IMF）心存疑虑，但在20世纪90年代，它的使用速度比互联网更快，萨尔瓦多已成为第一个将其与美元一起作为法定货币的国家。
佩特森说，人们想要比特币，采矿业通过税收和就业提供经济回报。此外，采矿业还可以为在特定时间和季节从水力、风能或太阳能获得过剩可再生能源的国家提供一种方式，使其在本国境内实现价值，而无需转移成本。据估计，采矿业使用的可再生能源比例在25%到57%之间。
“采矿本身并没有污染，”佩特森说。“如果你经营煤炭来经营采矿，那就另当别论了，这是你不想要的。采矿应该在挪威这样的地方进行——这可以成为一种节省被困能源的方法。例如，在挪威北部有过剩的能源，或者在萨尔瓦多，他们现在正在使用来自火山的能源，在必要时开始生产以前没有。"
Kryptovault的目标是到今年秋天让15000名矿工开始工作。37岁的奥托·希姆（Otto Him）和26岁的马丁·米卡森（Martin Mikalsen）管理着赫内福斯（Hønefoss）矿，他们将两堵巨大的墙之间的一个区域，即6500台计算机的吊舱，昵称为“大教堂”，以表彰其惊人的规模。
该公司正在从较老的比特币矿商过渡到从中国运来的新矿商，据说这些矿商的效率要高出三倍。将会有更多的废热被利用。该公司正在就为当地一家公司干燥海草进行初步谈判。佩特森说，叙事将发生变化。
“在某个时刻，一个又一个国家将采用比特币作为法定货币，在某个时刻，它显然将改变世界，”他说。“很明显，互联网将继续存在。”
*由于使用VPN或代理服务重定向IP地址，活动证据可能会膨胀。
数据来自剑桥替代金融中心的剑桥比特币电力消费指数。表基于2021年8月的最新数据。
note_en:
A  line of large blue skips full of chopped wood sit at the back of a site belonging to Norway’s biggest bitcoin mining operation, a 5,000 sq metre warehouse on the outskirts of Hønefoss, a small town 40 miles west of Oslo.
 Hot air is being pumped into the 12 skips through bendy corrugated pipes curling out from the warehouse. Despite the snow, it will take a few days for the logs to be dried out, after which a local lumberjack, grateful for the free service, will take them away for sale.
  The wood is being warmed by some of the so-called waste heat being emitted from thousands of stacked-high computer servers, known as miners, working away inside the warehouse. It is one of two such sites owned by the Norwegian company Kryptovault. The company expects its mining to account for just under 1% of the computing and process power in the global bitcoin network later this year.
 Bitcoin mining, the process of earning cryptocurrency by solving complex computational math puzzles and verifying transactions in the process, is famously energy-intensive. The latest calculation from Cambridge University’s  bitcoin electricity consumption index suggests that as a result, the sector consumes more energy in a year than many countries, including Argentina, Pakistan and Poland.
 Heat is an inevitable waste byproduct. Despite noise from ventilation fans so loud that the company had to spend about £1.5m on insulation after complaints from neighbours, the hot areas in the Hønefoss warehouse can reach 55C.
  For many, at a time of rocketing energy prices, this may be further evidence of the unsustainability of a business that was recently described by Robert McCauley, a senior fellow at Boston University’s global development policy centre, as “worse than a  Madoff-style Ponzi scheme”.
 The critics are certainly circling. Russia’s central bank has proposed outlawing all cryptocurrency operations in the country, and  China has already done so. Swedish regulators have called for something similar in Europe. Norway’s regional development minister, Bjørn Arild Gram, told the Guardian his government was reviewing its options.
 “Although crypto-mining and its underlying technology might represent some possible benefits in the long run, it is difficult to justify the extensive use of renewable energy today,” Gram said. “The ministry of local government and regional development is currently reviewing potential policy measures in order to address the challenges related to extensive energy usage caused by crypto-mining.”
 But there is a fightback brewing in the sector, of which Kryptovault, which uses only renewable energy, is a part. This week, the billionaire Michael Novogratz, the owner of Galaxy Digital, a digital assets company that seeks to become “the Goldman Sachs of crypto”, launched a sustainability programme on energy use and social responsibility, saying the industry needed to tackle a “false narrative around it being bad for the environment”.
 It has been described by some critics as greenwashing, but within a world that has until now been less than transparent over its activities, preferring secrecy over sympathy, this was a shift.
 Kjetil Hove Pettersen, 39, Kryptovault’s chief executive, who founded the company with friends after deciding to turn a hobby into a business, said he also believed it was time for the sector to push back at the prevailing narrative.
 “If you look at the total energy cost, globally, for any given thing, it’s always going to be huge – I think we can always compare to that of a small European country,” he said. “That includes also traditional gold mining, which takes more than four times the amount of energy as bitcoin mining.”
  Advocates for bitcoin argue that it offers the opportunity to exchange value with someone instantly without using a third party such as a bank, without permission and virtually for free. They say it is being adopted at a faster rate than the internet in the 1990s, with El Salvador having become the first country to  adopt it as legal tender, alongside the US dollar, in spite of misgivings from the International Monetary Fund.
 Pettersen said people wanted bitcoin, and mining operations provided economic returns through tax and employment. Mining is further said to offer a way for countries with excess renewable energy supply from hydro, wind or solar power at certain times and seasons to make value of it within their own borders and without transfer costs. Estimates of what proportion of the energy used in mining is renewable vary from 25% to 57%.
 “Mining is not polluting in itself,” said Pettersen. “If you are running coal to run mining then that’s another story, that’s what you don’t want. Mining should be done in more than places like Norway – and it can be a way to save trapped energy. For example, in northern Norway where there is excess, or in El Salvador where they are now using energy from volcanoes, setting up production when it wasn’t there before.”
 Kryptovault aims to have 15,000 miners at work by this autumn. Otto Him, 37 and Martin Mikalsen, 26, who manage the Hønefoss mine, have nicknamed one area between two huge walls, or pods, of 6,500 computers as “the cathedral”, in recognition of its awesome scale.
 The company is transitioning from older bitcoin miners to new ones shipped from China that are said to be three times more efficient. There will be plenty more waste heat to use. The company is in early talks to dry seaweed for a local firm. Pettersen says the narrative will change.
 “At some point, country after country will adopt bitcoin as legal tender and at some point it will be obvious that it is going to change the world,” he said. “Just as it became obvious that the internet was here to stay.”
   *Evidence of activity probably inflated owing to redirected IP addresses via the use of VPN or proxy services.
 Data from the Cambridge Centre for Alternative Finance’s Cambridge  bitcoin  electricity  consumption  index. Table based on the latest data from August 2021.
title: 比特币能否可持续？在挪威的矿井里，也可以干燥木材
title_en: Can Bitcoin be sustainable? Inside the Norwegian mine that also dries wood

软件开发人员已经发现了苹果新操作系统&#34；现实&#34；应用商店内上传日志和公司使用的GitHub存储库。
开发者Rens Verhoeven和Steve Troughton Smith在Twitter上广泛分享了这些参考资料。Verhoeven在推特上写道：
苹果的RealityOS在应用商店上传日志中做什么？AR/VR确认了吗？照片。啁啾com/wp7xwieeu
-Rens Verhoeven（@renssies）2022年1月17日
这条推文还附有一张日志截图，其中包括&#34；通用域名格式。苹果站台现实&#34；除了对现有平台的类似参考&#34；通用域名格式。苹果站台watchos"
当引用NicolásÁlvarez在推特上对GitHub回购发现的调用时，特劳顿·史密斯在推特上发布了以下内容：
“#如果目标_特性_REALITYOS”那么。这至少证实了它有自己的操作系统&amp；二进制文件，2）有一个realityOS模拟器https://t.co/6a25kWshXR照片。啁啾com/ryf5go5gfjg
-史蒂夫·特劳顿·史密斯（@stroughtonsmith）2022年2月9日
该推文附带的屏幕截图包括&#34；//等代码注释允许iOS可执行文件使用realityOS dylibs&#34；和&#34；//允许iOS_Sim可执行文件使用realityOS_Sim动态库"
然而，苹果正在一个混合现实平台上工作这一事实并不是什么新鲜事。基于苹果公司的工作列表&#39；苹果的职业网站、大量可靠的报告和其他来源，苹果&#39；s的混合现实平台已经开发多年，拥有数千人的团队。
然而，混合现实平台面临着几次延迟。例如，彭博社在2017年报道称，苹果&#39；苹果的首款耳机预定于2019年发布。该报告称苹果公司&#39；s为混合现实设备定制的操作系统#34；罗斯"
明智国等分析人士和彭博社等记者&#39；s的马克·古尔曼（Mark Gurman）一直声称高端混合现实耳机已经面世一段时间了。一些报道称，今年将推出一款耳机，但另一些报道称，耳机可能会在2023年推出。细节是模糊和矛盾的。
例如，Kuo曾表示，该设备将配备一个与M1相当的处理器。这表明它可能是独立的，并且赢得了&#39；附近不需要iPhone或Mac电脑。但最近的另一份报告称，苹果计划依靠附近的移动设备进行处理。
其中一些差异可能是因为苹果正在开发至少两种不同的设备。其中一款有望成为高端、支持虚拟现实技术的耳机，用于沉浸式内容&#39；这是今年或明年的预期。另一种设备是AR眼镜，可以在公共场所走动时佩戴。但该公司也可能仍在试验各种产品概念，其设计和特色尚未最终确定。
note_en:
Software developers have discovered apparent references to a new Apple operating system called "realityOS" in App Store upload logs and in GitHub repositories used by the company.
 The references were shared widely by developers Rens Verhoeven and Steve Troughton-Smith on Twitter. Verhoeven  tweeted:
 Uh what is Apple’s RealityOS doing in the App Store upload logs?  AR/VR confirmed?  pic.twitter.com/Wp7XWieeEU
— Rens Verhoeven (@renssies)  January 17, 2022
 The tweet was accompanied by a screenshot from the logs that included "com.apple.platform.realityos" alongside a similar reference for an existing platform, "com.apple.platform.watchos."
 And while quote-tweeting a callout of the  GitHub repo find  by Nicolás Álvarez, Troughton-Smith  tweeted the following:
 “ #if TARGET_FEATURE_REALITYOS”  Well then. This at least confirms it 1) has its own OS & binaries, and 2) has a realityOS Simulator  https://t.co/6a25kWshXR  pic.twitter.com/RyF5O5gFjg
— Steve Troughton-Smith (@stroughtonsmith)  February 9, 2022
 The screenshots accompanying that tweet included code comments like "// allow iOS executables to use realityOS dylibs" and "// allow iOS_Sim executables to use realityOS_Sim dylibs."
 The fact that Apple is working on a mixed reality platform is not new news, however. Based on job listings on Apple's careers site, numerous reliable reports, and other sources, Apple's mixed reality platform has been in development for years, with a team of thousands.
    The mixed reality platform has faced several delays, however. For example, Bloomberg reported back in 2017 that Apple's first headset was targeting a 2019 release date. That report called Apple's custom operating system for mixed reality devices " rOS."
 Analysts like Ming-chi Kuo and reporters like Bloomberg's Mark Gurman have been claiming that a high-end mixed reality headset has been coming for a while. Some reports said a headset would come this year, but others recently claimed that one could arrive in 2023 instead. Details are fuzzy and contradictory.
 For example,  Kuo has said that the device will feature an M1-equivalent processor of its own. This suggests that it may be standalone and won't require an iPhone or Mac nearby. But  another recent report claimed that Apple plans to rely on processing on a nearby mobile device.
 Some of these differences could be because Apple is working on at least two different devices. One is expected to be a high-end, VR-capable headset for immersive content—that's the one expected this year or next. Another device would be AR glasses meant to be worn while walking around public spaces. But the company could also still be experimenting with a variety of product concepts, with the designs and distinguishing features yet to be finalized.
title: 苹果的“realityOS”出现在GitHub提交、应用商店日志中
title_en: Apple’s “realityOS” surfaces in GitHub commits, App Store logs

note: 在过去的几个月里，我&#39；我从我的一个朋友那里学吉他，他碰巧是一位出色的老师。我很幸运。我在几年前就开始打球了，但可能只保留了我所知道的10%，所以我和任何一个几乎没有经验的球员一样接近于从零开始。基本上，我可以涉猎并弹奏一些和弦，但之后我的技能很快就稳定下来了。它&#39；这是一个孤独、荒凉、杂草丛生的平坦高原。你没有&#39；在那里闲逛不会更好。因此&#39；这就是为什么我&#39；我回到了面对面的课程。我试过YouTube，我试过一些书，我试过一两个应用，我试过很多东西，但我&#39；我发现我只是通过一对一的面对面指导来学习吉他。而我&#39；我好多了。但我&#39；我也在那里发现了&#39；这是另一个与实践相结合的维度：做自己想做的事情感觉更好&#39；重新学习。人们很容易将感觉更好视为“变得更好”的孪生兄弟。但我&#39；我在那里找到了&#39；这是不同的。变得更好是为了改善，感觉更好是为了自信和舒适。它&#39；这是一种伴随着变得更好而发展起来的放松。我对吉他本身感觉更好——它&#39；它不再是一个吓人的东西。当然，我&#39；我还有1000英里要走才能到达我想去的地方，但我&#39；我已经通过了第一个关卡：打球感觉更好，即使我打得不好。我发现自己更频繁地拿起吉他。不是真正的练习，而是真正的胡闹。现在我感觉好多了，我&#39；我给了自己更多的自由去享受它。更多的技能带来更多的空间，但更好的感觉带来更多的心灵空间。我记得90年代中期我在学习网页设计时也有过类似的感觉。一开始很难。我没有&#39；我不喜欢它，因为我是&#39；我一点也不擅长。我在学习，我能做东西，但我没有&#39；我还不舒服。我没有&#39；我没有掌握足够的技能来放松。然后，最终，我做到了。我喜欢它！我有点喜欢。在那一刻，我几乎没有更多的知识，但我有一种感觉，我能做到这一点，我想做到这一点，我做这件事感觉很好。这使我有机会进一步学习。我&#39；我开始相信这是第一个值得为之奋斗的里程碑。对自己的事情感觉良好&#39；你在干什么。不放一首歌，不跑一段距离，不设计一页，不把一个完美的罐子扔到轮子上，或者其他什么——只是享受你所做的事情&#39；你在干什么。一旦你击中它，你&#39；我们正在路上。
note_en: Over the past few months I've been taking guitar lessons from a friend of mine who happens to be an exceptional teacher. I got lucky.  I used to play years ago, but probably only retained 10% of what I knew, so I was as close to starting from scratch as any barely-experienced player could be. Basically, I could dabble and play some chords, but my skills plateaued quickly after that. And it's a lonely, desolate, tumbleweedy-flat plateau. You don't get better hanging out there.  So that's why I'm back to in-person lessons. I tried YouTube, I tried some books, I tried an app or two, I tried a bunch of stuff, but I've discovered I simply learn guitar best with one-on-one in-person instruction.  And I've gotten better. But I've also discovered there's another dimension that unlocks with practice:  feeling better doing the thing you're learning.  It would be easy to dismiss feeling better as the identical twin of getting better. But I've found there's a difference.  Getting better is about improving specifically, feeling better is about confidence and comfort. It's a certain ease that develops alongside getting better. I feel better about the guitar itself — it's no longer this intimidating object. Granted, I've got 1000 miles to go before I get where I want to be with it, but I've passed the first waypoint: It just  feels better to play, even when I play poorly.  I find myself picking up the guitar more frequently. Not to literally practice, but to literally fuck around. Now that I feel better about it, I've given myself more latitude to have some fun with it. More skills bring more headroom, but better feels bring more heartroom.  I remember a similar feeling when I was learning web design way back in the mid-90s. At first it was hard. And I didn't like it because I wasn't any good at it. I was learning, I could make stuff, but I didn't feel comfortable yet. I hadn't pocketed enough skills to feel at ease. And then, eventually, I did. And I liked it! I kinda loved it. I barely had more knowledge under my belt in that very moment, but I had a sense that  I could do this, I want to do this, I feel good doing this. It opened me up to further learning.  I've come to believe this is the first milestone worth shooting for. Feeling good about the thing you're doing. Not playing a song, or running a distance, or designing a page, or throwing a perfect pot on the wheel, or whatever — but simply  enjoying the thing you're doing. Once you hit that, you're on your way.
title: 变得更好还是感觉更好

note:
没有人喜欢华盛顿特区。甚至在它建成之前，反联邦主义的纽约州参议员托马斯·特雷德威尔（Thomas Tredwell）就把它称为“政治蜂巢，社会上所有的无人机都将被收集起来，以土地上的蜂蜜为食。”财政部长小奥利弗·沃尔科特（Oliver Wolcott Jr.）在1800年写道，“大多数居民都是下流人，他们的外表表明他们是邪恶和放纵的。”查尔斯·狄更斯在1842年看到了“一群绝望的冒险家”，他们“使政治斗争变得如此激烈和残酷……以至于敏感和敏感的人应该远离。”马克·吐温和查尔斯·达德利·华纳在1873年的小说《镀金时代》（The Gilded Age）中将华盛顿视为失业工人的永久倾倒地，称之为“为无助者提供的古老而仁慈的国家避难所”亨利·亚当斯（Henry Adams）将这座内战后的城市描述为“一个纯粹的政治阵营”，他1880年的小说《民主》（Democracy）将华盛顿描述为腐败的炖锅。
2013年，记者马克·莱博维奇（Mark Leibovich）在这个小镇上提出了最新的批评：两个派对、一个葬礼，外加大量的代客泊车-在美国镀金的首都。这本书巧妙地描绘了这个国家的首都，为富有的说客、追逐镜头的国会议员、自以为是的电视和印刷媒体记者，以及各种短视的地位寻求者提供了一个游戏场所，莱博维奇将这些人统称为俱乐部。莱博维奇写道：“华盛顿可能不能很好地为国家服务，但事实上，它为华盛顿本身发挥了出色的作用——一个由美丽忙碌的人们不断书写自己生活故事的城市。”
这本书的书名选得很好。在1983年哈珀关于说客安妮·韦克斯勒（Anne Wexler）的一篇令人难忘的简介中，记者戴维·欧文（David Owen）思考了这句话精心打造的虚伪：
“这个城镇。”这就是像韦克斯勒这样有影响力的华盛顿人几乎总是提到他们的城市。这个表达完美地体现了他们喜欢把自己看作是常识性实用主义者的方式，对他们来说，政治是一种介于种植苜蓿和从事专业运动之间的民间称呼。
作家兼克林顿的白宫助手西德尼·布卢门塔尔（Sidney Blumenthal）在1995年的讽刺剧《这座城市》（This Town）中讽刺了这句话。莱博维奇在写完这本书时才知道这座城市的存在。布卢门塔尔要求莱博维奇将他的书称为其他东西，尽管书名不受版权保护，因为他的剧本是“短语和概念的起源”，这并不完全正确。这促使莱博维奇在他的书中回答说，布卢门塔尔的戏剧“几乎被所有人遗忘，无论是在‘这个’还是任何城镇”这也不完全正确；洛杉矶剧院作品的录音可以通过音频设备随时获取。（非常好！）
布卢门塔尔·莱博维奇的口角模仿了莱博维奇在这个小镇上嘲讽的方式：两名俱乐部成员就一些只对俱乐部重要的事情交换意见。NPR和《每日野兽》报道了这场争吵。事实上，2019冠状病毒疾病研究中心比Barney Graham和Kizzmekia S. Corbett在2020年1月建立了CVID-19疫苗的蓝图时更受关注。
这两名联邦工作人员最终受到了公众的关注。例如，劳伦斯怀特的2021本书《瘟疫年：科威特时代的美国》，一篇摘自《纽约客》的文章，提到了Graham，2021年12月《时代》杂志的一篇文章将Graham和科贝特命名为科威特研究者，他们是“年度英雄”。但这个故事花了一两年时间才得以突破，这无疑是因为，在被告知华盛顿什么都做不好的40年后，公众迟迟不相信它能创造出任何有用的东西。一开始是保守派的观点（“政府就是问题”），后来变成了一种非政治的真理（华盛顿“为华盛顿自己出色地工作”）。格雷厄姆和科比特不符合这种模式。他们不是俱乐部的成员。
1900年至1929年间，小罗伯特·佩勒姆（Robert Pelham Jr.）是人口普查局（Bureau of the Census）的一名办事员，在伍德罗·威尔逊（Woodrow Wilson）为重新安置联邦劳动力所做的努力中幸存下来。在人口普查时，佩勒姆发明了两台机器，极大地提高了统计局工作的速度和准确性：一台将纸条粘贴到数据表上的设备，以及一台计票装置。
莱博维奇将这座城市描绘成华盛顿的一幅睿智全景。但这只是华盛顿人渴望接受《纽约时报》记者采访的全景：国会议员、一线说客、党的女招待、有线电视新闻人物、国会山上更具进取心的工作人员，以及古怪的白宫助手。我慷慨地说，他们的人数是3000人。（参加一年一度的白宫记者晚宴的人数最多为2670人，几乎所有想去的人都能参加。）
莱博维奇的华盛顿有个问题。他的调查几乎排除了大都会地区大约28.3万名公务员；几乎所有172000名军事人员；华盛顿特区36700名工人中的大多数；它的59.1万名做好事者中的大多数都为非营利组织工作。这意味着超过100万人生活在一个人口为67万的首都及其周边地区。为了纪念约翰·F·肯尼迪总统的就职请求，“不要问你的国家能为你做些什么，而要问你能为你的国家做些什么”，让我们称他们为“阿斯纽特”
阿斯纽特一家是我四十年来在华盛顿的邻居。（除了一些准备有线电视的记者外，我没有足够的钱与俱乐部打交道。）现役军人更难了解，因为他们住在军事基地，但十几年前，当我和五角大楼的“dazdee”（国防部副助理部长，拼写为DASD）住在一起时，我在社交上认识了我的那一份。作为一个部落，Asknots正直、善良、能干、有公益精神。当然也有例外。但总的来说，Asknots正是你喜欢的邻居。我很幸运有很多朋友。
从广义上讲，Asknots家族是在20世纪30年代蜂拥而至的摇摆不定的新政自由派的继承人。和他们一样，Asknots一家来到华盛顿，希望让这个国家和世界变得更美好。他们不是苦行僧；他们中的大多数人生活舒适，如果他们愿意，许多人可能会辞去工作，加入美国百分之一人口的行列。大多数人不想这么做。
阿斯纽特一家被各种各样地描述为“新阶层”，被称为“永久的华盛顿”，更不祥的是，被称为“深州”，尽管并非所有人都从政府那里领工资。在马加忠诚的狂热者中，2021年1月6日袭击国会大厦的那种人，在他们试图不让唐纳德·特朗普和他的军团践踏现有的民主治理结构的情况下，打击报复他们的敌人。起义者没有错。在特朗普执政的四年里，是阿斯纽特家族用口香糖和铁丝网把政府团结在一起。在很多方面，他们拯救了共和国。正因为如此，我永远无法理解现实美国的所有人，当我说我来自华盛顿时，他们可怜地看着我，告诉我，在那可怕的沼泽地里生活在所有这些可怕的人中间，一定是一场多么艰难的考验。
我们能澄清一下吗？华盛顿不是，也从来不是沼泽。这不是一片真正的沼泽。波托马克河的一条支流叫做鹅溪，后来改名为台伯溪，沿着现在的宪法大道。污水被倒入其中，对公众健康造成危害，因此在19世纪末被掩盖起来。波托马克河本身在19世纪中期因上游农业而淤塞；造成了一些洪水，但通过建造反射池和潮汐盆地，问题得以解决。（波托马克河从哥伦比亚特区到切萨皮克湾的河段是潮汐的。）这样的土木工程在19世纪的大城市里是司空见惯的；如果说华盛顿是建在沼泽地上的，那么纽约、波士顿、芝加哥，以及几乎所有其他在主要水道周围或附近修建的大都市也是如此。
但更重要的是，它也不是一个隐喻性的沼泽。在纽约、洛杉矶、旧金山和休斯敦，美国货币文化的过度过剩更为明显。权力斗争发生在华盛顿，但也发生在匹兹堡、得梅因、圣达菲和其他人类居住的地方。嘲笑未受教育的大众，这是MAGA类型的人想象中的华盛顿特区最喜欢的消遣，实际上在华盛顿比我能想到的任何一个大城市都要少；通常取而代之的恶习，对美国人民深层智慧的某种虔诚，相比之下，是相当温和的。是的，有一些愤世嫉俗的公司说客希望破坏税法，但也有很多正派的人在发自肺腑地游说，希望获得更实惠的住房或更清洁的空气，并为他们的麻烦支付可能8万美元。华盛顿对那些想让世界变得更美好的热心公益人士来说，比对那些想让全球陷入碳排放和多代家庭信任的卑鄙人士来说，更具有吸引力。（下流的人通常在其他地方做得更好。）在罗伯特·肯尼迪（Robert Kennedy）对乔治·萧伯纳（George Bernard Shaw）的著名重新表述中，华盛顿是一个人们“梦想着从未有过的事情，并问为什么不存在”的地方这些梦想中有些是可信的，有些则不然。但我从未见过其他地方有如此多的理想主义者允许他们对一个更好的社会的愿景来管理他们的日常生活。如果你不来，就没什么意义了。
Frances Oldham Kelsey在食品和药物管理局工作了45年多。在1960年工作的第一个月里，她审查了沙利度胺的一份药物申请。她顶住制药行业的巨大压力，拒绝了FDA的批准。两年内，有报道称沙利度胺导致了可怕的出生缺陷。
华盛顿特区是以其第一任总统命名的，但它主要是由第三十二任总统建造的。即使是一个不速之客也会注意到20世纪三四十年代的政府大楼。实际上，这座城市应该被称为Rooseveltland，D.C.从1930年罗斯福上台前三年到1950年，即他去世后五年，哥伦比亚特区的人口从486869人上升到历史最高点802178人。珍珠港事件前夕，奥尔登·史蒂文斯在《哈珀》杂志上报道说，每个月都有5000多名新的联邦工作人员抵达这座城市。他们中的许多人是今天的阿斯纽特的先驱。
当时和现在一样，俱乐部不知道该如何看待他们。当时的俱乐部并不是由绿屋习惯的人组成，而是被称为“穴居者”的蓝血女主人，她们将自己的地区血统追溯到最早的时代，并震惊地发现新的经销商不是穿着晚礼服，而是穿着西装来到他们的聚会。“教授们”在星期六晚邮报的一篇1936篇社论中嗤之以鼻，“他们太专注于重要的事情而不用花十分钟换换衣服……即使是女主人，常年去寻找所需要的额外的人，也很少考虑在这个按字母顺序排列的机构里画画。”
这些外星人是谁？“他们是社会工作者、农场经济学家、自由派律师、工会组织者，”长期担任电视新闻记者的戴维·布林克利在1988年的《华盛顿开战》一书中写道，
所有这些都是政治脊骨治疗师，他们渴望用大拇指触碰国家的脊骨，折断它，直到血液再次流向美国生活的各个极端，让它恢复健康和繁荣。
行善者的迁移从未停止。第二次世界大战后，联邦劳动力稳定在大约200万人左右，这大约是目前的水平，但Asknots不断到来，有时轮流获得奖学金或其他临时任务，有时为该地区新兴的非政府组织（NGO）部门工作，有时在特区政府工作，有时为政府承包商工作。（如今，与直接为联邦政府工作相比，从事政府合同和补助工作的人要多得多。）
Kizzmekia Corbett就是这些移民之一。科贝特是一名来自北卡罗来纳州希尔斯堡的36岁非裔美国女性，她在高中时决定要成为一名科学家。她曾就读于巴尔的摩县马里兰大学，在那里她主修生物科学和社会学，并在北卡罗来纳大学教堂山分校，在2014，她获得了博士学位的微生物学和免疫学博士学位。她写了一篇关于登革热人体抗体反应的博士论文，这是一种在热带和亚热带气候中常见的蚊子传播的疾病，并前往斯里兰卡对患有该疾病的儿童进行临床研究。毕业后，科比特来到华盛顿，成为美国国立卫生研究院疫苗研究中心的研究员。她来和巴尼·格雷厄姆一起工作。
国家过敏和传染病研究所所长安东尼·福奇说，格雷厄姆“比我认识的任何人都更了解疫苗学”，他在20年前招募格雷厄姆加入疫苗研究中心。科贝特在格雷厄姆读本科时就开始与她合作，这是美国国立卫生研究院（NIH）一个项目的一部分，该项目旨在识别来自弱势群体的天才年轻科学家。在她作为博士后回到格雷厄姆的实验室后，格雷厄姆让她构建刺突蛋白，并在小鼠身上测试产生的抗体。早在新冠病毒大流行之前，“她在冠状病毒方面做了很多基础工作，”格雷厄姆告诉我。
科贝特的努力奠基于2013，当时德克萨斯大学的Graham和Jason McLellan曾在NIH和Graham合作过，设计了一种疫苗，目前还没有批准用于儿童呼吸道合胞病毒（RSV）和中东呼吸综合征（MeRS）的儿科疾病。这项2019冠状病毒疾病的研究结果与设计CVID-19疫苗的结果是如此的相似，因为在中国发布了科威特基因序列后，Graham、McLellan和科贝特在其生产疫苗蓝图后仅24小时就被攻克。两个月后，摩德纳开始了临床试验。
辉瑞在两个月后开始临床试验。格雷厄姆、麦克莱伦和科贝特没有像他们与摩德纳那样直接与辉瑞合作，但辉瑞疫苗临床研究和开发高级副总裁威廉·格鲁伯受到格雷厄姆和麦克莱伦早期对RSV研究的强烈影响。事实上，格鲁伯和格雷厄姆在20世纪70年代初是赖斯大学的老朋友大学室友，后来是范德比尔特在纳什维尔的疫苗研究中心的同事和偶尔的合著者。2013年RSV取得突破后，格雷厄姆和妻子跳上车，从马里兰州洛克维尔的家开车到纽约州北部，向格鲁伯展示了这项研究。然后，格鲁伯派了一个团队前往马里兰州贝塞斯达国家卫生研究院校园40号楼二楼格雷厄姆的实验室，以了解更多信息。
当格雷厄姆得知辉瑞公司的新冠病毒疫苗首次获得批准，甚至比他希望的更有效时，他坐在办公桌前哭了起来。劳伦斯·赖特（Lawrence Wright）在《瘟疫年》（The鼠疫年）中写道：“自从父亲去世后，他就没有哭得那么厉害。”对科贝特来说，当摩德纳疫苗的结果出来时，他会流泪。“我觉得没有犯错的余地，”她告诉CNN。“否则，你就浪费了时间，人们就死了。”
非营利性公共服务合作组织（Partnership for Public Service）表彰了格雷厄姆（Graham）和科比特（Corbett）的成就，将他们评为年度联邦雇员。2021年5月，科贝特离开NIH成为哈佛大学免疫学和传染病学的助理教授。格雷厄姆于8月退休，并与妻子搬到亚特兰大，以便更接近孙辈。在他们中间，格雷厄姆和科贝特在政府的工资单上记录了四分之一个多世纪。
对懒惰的联邦雇员不屑一顾的讽刺激怒了格雷厄姆。他说：“当人们对‘哦，政府工作人员，九点到五点’发表评论时，我在政府中认识的大多数人，几乎所有我在政府中认识的人……都有一种使命感。”。根据记录，联邦雇员的实际标准工作日是8:30到5点，大多数格雷厄姆共事的联邦实验室工作人员“每周工作65小时左右”，因为否则“你真的无法取得进展”格雷厄姆来到美国国立卫生研究院时，他在自己的领域很有成就。他放弃了在研究型大学更赚钱的机会，在NIH“实时”研究公共卫生危机
在特朗普的领导下，阿斯纽特一家的日子很不好过。他经常在推特上把它们称为“深度状态”当总检察长的报告批评了他的政治任命或揭露了他更愿意否认的问题时，他肆意解雇了总检察长。他解雇或降职了国家安全委员会退役陆军中校亚历山大·温德曼和生物医学高级研究与发展局的里克·布莱特等说出真相的告密者。他解雇了联邦调查局特工彼得·斯特佐克，因为他犯下了不可原谅的罪行，在私人短信中称特朗普为“白痴”和“傻瓜”，并公开嘲笑斯特佐克与丽莎·佩奇的婚外情（“我爱你，丽莎！我太爱你了！”）。他罢免了玛丽·约瓦诺维奇，因为她正确地预测，特朗普向乌克兰施压抹黑亨特·拜登的努力会让他被弹劾。这些人中没有一个是想得到参加Gridiron Club晚宴的邀请。
特朗普还撤销或限制了监管，尤其是在环境方面，并试图（尽管大多失败）大幅削减机构预算。许多职业官员纷纷离职，尤其是劳工部，该部门失去了超过10%的劳动力；损失超过5%的州、教育和内政部；农业部损失了5%。那些留下来的人一直开着灯，即使特朗普试图把灯关掉。
克里斯蒂·格林就是这样一位英雄。格里姆自1999年起担任卫生和公共服务监察长办公室（IG）的官员，在科罗拉多州的埃杰沃特长大，她的祖父在那里管理公共工程部门，为小镇供水，修建新的道路和人行道，并维护城市公园。“我为他如何让我们的城市变得更好而自豪，”她后来在参议院财政委员会作证时回忆道。
2020年1月，格林被任命为HHS代理总监。三个月后，在大流行袭来后，她的办公室发布了一份对全国323家医院的调查，该调查是在3月的最后一周进行的。医院报告说，检测用品严重短缺，N95防护口罩普遍短缺，难以满足医院患者的需求。“这不是对2019冠状病毒疾病的HHS反应的回顾。”报告说。但特朗普当然认为这是个人侮辱。当一名记者在他每日的一次新冠病毒简报会上问他这件事时，他说，“这是错的”，并要求知道这位免疫球蛋白的名字。后来，他在推特上谴责她是党派人士（更不用说格林在乔治·W·布什总统手下工作了八年），并称这份报告是“另一份虚假档案”（指一名退休英国间谍的一份不可信文件，声称俄罗斯拥有有关特朗普的泄露信息）。几周后，特朗普用另一名从未得到参议院确认的代理检察长取代了格林。格林恢复了她以前担任副检察长的职务。乔·拜登总统将她重新提拔为代理检察长，并于6月提名她担任该职位

note_en:
Nobody has ever liked Washington, D.C. Even before it was built, Thomas Tredwell, an anti-Federalist New York state senator,  called  it a “political hive, where all the drones in the society are to be collected to feed on the honey of the land.” Treasury Secretary Oliver Wolcott Jr. wrote in 1800 that “most of the inhabitants are low people, whose appearance indicates vice and intemperance.” Charles Dickens  saw  in 1842 a “stream of desperate adventurers” who “make the strife of politics so fierce and brutal … that sensitive and delicate-minded persons shall be kept aloof.” Mark Twain and Charles Dudley Warner saw Washington as a  permanent dumping ground  for unemployable workers, calling it, in their 1873 novel,   The Gilded Age , “the grand old benevolent National Asylum for the Helpless.” Henry Adams remembered the post–Civil War city as “a mere political camp,” and his 1880 novel,   Democracy , portrayed Washington as a stewpot of corruption.
 In 2013, the journalist Mark Leibovich brought this critique up to date in   This Town: Two Parties and a Funeral—Plus Plenty of Valet Parking!—in America’s Gilded Capital. The book presented a deft portrait of the nation’s capital as a playpen for rich lobbyists, camera-chasing members of Congress, self-regarding TV and print journalists, and assorted myopic status-seekers that Leibovich called, collectively, the Club. “Washington may not serve the country well,” Leibovich wrote, “but it has in fact worked splendidly for Washington itself—a city of beautifully busy people constantly writing the story of their own lives.”
 The book’s title was well-chosen. In a  memorable 1983  Harper’s profile of the lobbyist Anne Wexler, the journalist David Owen pondered the phrase’s finely wrought phoniness:
  “This town.” That’s how influential Washingtonians like Wexler almost always refer to their city. The expression perfectly embodies the way they like to think of themselves, as commonsensical pragmaticians for whom politics is a folksy calling that falls somewhere between growing alfalfa and playing a professional sport.
 The writer and Clinton White House aide Sidney Blumenthal  lampooned the phrase in his satirical 1995 play,  This Town, of whose existence Leibovich learned only as he was finishing up his book. Blumenthal demanded that Leibovich call his book something else, even though a title can’t be copyrighted, because his play was “the origin of the phrase and concept,” which wasn’t exactly right. That prompted Leibovich to answer, in his book, that Blumenthal’s play was “forgotten by nearly everyone, in ‘this’ or any town.” That wasn’t exactly right either; an L.A. Theatre Works recording is  readily available on Audible. (It’s pretty good!)
 The Blumenthal-Leibovich spat mimicked what Leibovich lampooned in  This Town: two Club members exchanging barbs over something that mattered only to the Club. The quarrel was covered by  NPR and  The Daily Beast. It received more attention, in fact, than Barney Graham and Kizzmekia S. Corbett of the National Institutes of Health received in January 2020 when they built the blueprint for the Covid-19 vaccine.
  Publicity eventually caught up with these two federal workers. For instance, Lawrence Wright’s 2021 book,   The Plague Year: America in the Time of Covid, an  excerpt of which ran in  The New Yorker, mentions Graham, and a December 2021  Time magazine article names Graham and Corbett among Covid researchers who were “ Heroes of the Year.” But the story took a year or two to break through, no doubt because, after four decades of being told Washington can do nothing right, the public was slow to believe it could create anything useful. What began as a conservative bromide (“Government is the problem”) hardened into an apolitical truism (Washington “worked splendidly for Washington itself”). Graham and Corbett didn’t fit the paradigm. They weren’t members of the Club.
   Robert Pelham Jr. was a clerk at the Bureau of the Census from 1900 to 1929, where he survived Woodrow Wilson’s efforts to resegregate the federal workforce. While at Census, Pelham invented two machines that vastly improved the speed and accuracy of the bureau’s work: an apparatus to paste slips of paper onto data sheets, and a tallying device.
  Leibovich presented  This Town as a wised-up panorama of Washington. But it was a panorama only of those Washingtonians eager to be interviewed by a reporter for  The New York Times: members of Congress, A-list lobbyists, party hostesses, cable news personalities, the more pushy staffers on Capitol Hill, and the odd White House aide. I would put their number, generously, at 3,000. (The maximum crowd capacity at the annual White House Correspondents’ Dinner, attended by pretty much anybody who wants to go, is 2,670.)
  Here’s the problem with Leibovich’s Washington. His survey excluded virtually all the metropolitan area’s roughly 283,000  civil servants; virtually all its 172,000  military personnel; most of its 36,700 D.C.  city workers; and most of its 591,000  do-gooders working for nonprofit organizations. That’s more than a million people, living in and around a capital whose population is 670,000. Let’s call them the Asknots, in memory of President John F. Kennedy’s inaugural plea, “Ask not what your country can do for you, ask what you can do for your country.”
 The Asknots are the people I’ve known as neighbors in Washington over four decades. (I’m not rich enough to rub shoulders with the Club, except for some of the cable-ready journalists.) Active-duty military personnel are a little harder to know, because they live on military bases, but I met socially my share a dozen years ago while I was living with a Pentagon “dazdee” (deputy assistant secretary of defense, spelled DASD). As a tribe, Asknots are upright, kind, capable, and public-spirited. There are exceptions, of course. But in general, Asknots are exactly the sort of people you like to have as neighbors. I’m fortunate to count many as friends.
  Broadly speaking, the Asknots are heirs to the wonky New Deal liberals who flocked to the city during the 1930s. Like them, the Asknots came to Washington wanting to make the country and the world a better place. They aren’t ascetics; they live comfortably, most of them, and, if they wanted, many could likely quit their jobs and join the ranks of America’s one percenters. Most don’t want to.
 The Asknots have been described variously as “the New Class,” as “ permanent Washington,” and, more ominously, as the “ Deep State,” though not all of them draw paychecks from the government. The more fanatical among the MAGA faithful—the sort who  stormed the Capitol on January 6, 2021—judge the Asknots their enemy insofar as they tried not to let Donald Trump and his legions trample over existing structures of democratic governance. The insurrectionists aren’t wrong. It was the Asknots who held the government together, during four long years under Trump, with chewing gum and baling wire. In many ways, they saved the Republic. The Asknots are why I can never understand all those people out there in the Real America who look at me pityingly when I say I’m from Washington and tell me what a trial it must be to live among all those awful people in that dreadful swamp.
 Can we clear something up? Washington isn’t, and  never was, a swamp. It isn’t a literal swamp. A tributary of the Potomac called Goose Creek, later renamed Tiber Creek, ran along what is now Constitution Avenue. Sewage was dumped into it, it became a public health hazard, so in the late nineteenth century it was  covered up. The Potomac itself silted up in the mid–nineteenth century due to farming upriver; some flooding resulted, but that was resolved through construction of the Reflecting Pool and the Tidal Basin. (The stretch of the Potomac from D.C. to the Chesapeake Bay is tidal.) Such civil engineering was routine in major cities during the nineteenth century; if Washington is built on a swamp, then so are New York, Boston, Chicago, and just about every other metropolis built around or near a major waterway.
 But more important, it isn’t a metaphorical swamp either. The gross excesses of America’s money culture are much more pronounced in New York, Los Angeles, San Francisco, and Houston. Power struggles take place in Washington, but they take place, too, in Pittsburgh, Des Moines, Santa Fe, and everyplace else where humans dwell. Sneering at the untutored masses, something MAGA types imagine to be D.C.’s favorite pastime, is actually far less common in Washington than in any major city I can think of; the vice that usually takes its place, a certain piousness about the deeper wisdom of the American People, is, by comparison, pretty benign. Yes, there are cynical corporate lobbyists looking to corrupt the tax code, but there are also plenty of decent folks lobbying their hearts out for more affordable housing or cleaner air and pulling down maybe $80,000 for their troubles. Washington is a much more powerful magnet for public-spirited people who want to make the world a better place than it is for sleazy people who want to drown the globe in carbon emissions and multigenerational family trusts. (The sleazy types can usually do better elsewhere.) Washington is a place where people, in Robert Kennedy’s famous reformulation of George Bernard Shaw, “dream things that never were and ask why not.” Some of those dreams are plausible, and some are not. But I’ve never seen anyplace else where so many idealistic people allow their visions of a better society to govern their everyday lives. There isn’t much point coming here if you don’t.
  Frances Oldham Kelsey worked more than 45 years at the Food and Drug Administration. During her first month on the job in 1960, she reviewed a drug application for thalidomide. Resisting heavy pressure from the drug industry, she denied FDA approval. Within two years, reports surfaced that thalidomide caused horrific birth defects.
  Washington, D.C., is named for its first president, but it was built mostly by its thirty-second. Even a casual visitor won’t fail to notice the profusion of government buildings dating to the 1930s and 1940s. Really, the city should be called Rooseveltland, D.C. Between 1930, three years before Roosevelt assumed office, and 1950, five years after he died, the District of Columbia’s population rose from 486,869 to its all-time peak of 802,178. On the eve of Pearl Harbor, Alden Stevens  reported in  Harper’s that more than 5,000 new federal workers were arriving in the city each month. Many of them were forerunners of today’s Asknots.
 Then as now, the Club didn’t know what to make of them. The Club in those days consisted not of green-room habitués but of blue-blooded hostesses known as “cave dwellers” who traced their district lineage back to its earliest days and were appalled to observe that New Dealers arrived at their soirées not in dinner jackets but in business suits. “Professors,” sniffed a 1936 society column in  The Saturday Evening Post, “are too preoccupied with important matters to spare ten minutes for changing their clothes…. Even hostesses perennially in search of the necessary extra man rarely consider drawing upon this army of alphabetical agencies.”
 Who were these aliens? “They were social workers, farm economists, liberal lawyers, union organizers,” the longtime television newsman David Brinkley wrote in his delightful 1988 history,  Washington Goes to War,
 all of them political chiropractors eager to get their thumbs on the national spine, to snap it and crack it until the blood again flowed outward to all the extremities of American life, returning it to health and prosperity.
 This in-migration of do-gooders never stopped. After World War II, the federal workforce  stabilized at around two million, which is about where is remains today, but the Asknots kept coming, sometimes rotating in and out on fellowships or other temporary assignments, sometimes working for the area’s burgeoning nongovernmental organization (NGO) sector, sometimes working in D.C. local government, and sometimes working for government contractors. (Many more people today work on government contracts and grants than work directly for the federal government.)
  Kizzmekia Corbett was one of these migrants.  Corbett is a 36-year-old African American woman from Hillsborough, North Carolina, who  decided in high school that she wanted to be a scientist. She was educated at the University of Maryland, Baltimore County, where she majored in biological sciences and sociology, and at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, where in 2014 she received her Ph.D. in microbiology and immunology. She wrote her doctoral thesis on human antibody responses to dengue fever, a mosquito-borne disease common in tropical and subtropical climates, and traveled to Sri Lanka to perform clinical research with children who suffered from the disease. After she graduated, Corbett came to Washington to be a research fellow at the National Institutes of Health’s Vaccine Research Center. She came to work with Barney Graham.
 Graham “understands vaccinology better than anyone I know,”  according to Anthony Fauci, director of the National Institute of Allergy and Infectious Diseases, who  recruited Graham to the Vaccine Research Center two decades ago. Corbett worked with Graham starting when she was an undergraduate, as part of a program at NIH to identify gifted young scientists from disadvantaged backgrounds. After she returned to Graham’s lab as a postdoc, he put her to work building spike proteins and testing the resultant antibodies on mice. Well before the Covid pandemic, “she was really doing a lot of the basic work on coronaviruses,” Graham told me.
 The foundation for Corbett’s efforts was laid in 2013 when Graham and Jason McLellan of the University of Texas, who’d worked previously with Graham at NIH,  designed vaccines, neither yet approved, for a childhood disease called respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) and Middle East Respiratory Syndrome (MERS). This work, and subsequent work with Moderna on the  Zika virus, turned out to be so relevant to designing a vaccine for Covid-19 that it took Graham, McLellan, and Corbett  only 24 hours after China posted Covid’s genetic sequence online to produce their vaccine blueprint. Two months later, Moderna started its clinical trials.
  Pfizer began clinical trials two months after that. Graham, McLellan, and Corbett didn’t work directly with Pfizer, as they did with Moderna, but William Gruber, Pfizer’s senior vice president of vaccine clinical research and development, was strongly influenced by Graham and McLellan’s earlier research on RSV. As it happened, Gruber and Graham were  old friends—college roommates at Rice University in the early 1970s and later colleagues and occasional co-authors at Vanderbilt’s vaccine research center in Nashville. After 2013’s RSV breakthrough, Graham had jumped with his wife into their car and driven from their home in Rockville, Maryland, to upstate New York, where he showed the research to Gruber. Gruber then sent a team to Graham’s lab on the second floor of Building 40 on the NIH campus in Bethesda, Maryland—right smack in the “swamp”—to learn more.
  When Graham received word that Pfizer’s Covid vaccine, which was approved first, was more effective even than he’d hoped, he sat down at his desk and wept. “He hadn’t cried that hard,” Lawrence Wright reports in  The Plague Year, “since his father died.” For Corbett, the  moment for tears was when the results came back on the Moderna vaccine. “I felt like there was no room for mistakes,” she told CNN. “Otherwise, you lost time and people died.”
 The nonprofit Partnership for Public Service recognized Graham and Corbett’s achievement by  naming them federal employees of the year. In May 2021, Corbett  left NIH to become an assistant professor of immunology and infectious diseases at Harvard. Graham retired in August and moved with his wife to Atlanta to be closer to the grandkids. Between them, Graham and Corbett logged more than a quarter-century on the government payroll.
 The dismissive caricature of the lazy federal employees irritates Graham. “When people make these comments about ‘Oh government workers, nine to five by the clock’— most people I knew in government, virtually all the people I knew in government … felt a sense of purpose,” he said. For the record, the actual standard day for a federal employee is 8:30 to 5, and most federal lab workers with whom Graham has worked put in “around 65 hours a week,” because otherwise “you really can’t make progress.” Graham was well-established in his field when he came to NIH. He passed up more lucrative opportunities at research universities to work at NIH on public health crises “in real time.”
  The Asknots had a  rough time under Trump. He abused them routinely on Twitter as the “deep state.” He fired inspectors general with abandon when their reports criticized his political appointees or revealed problems he preferred to deny. He  fired or  demoted truth-telling whistleblowers like retired Army Lt. Col. Alexander Vindman from the National Security Council and Rick Bright from the Biomedical Advanced Research and Development Authority. He  fired FBI agent Peter Strzok for the unpardonable sin of calling Trump an “idiot” and a “douche” in private text messages, and  publicly mocked Strzok’s extramarital affair with Lisa Page (“I love you, Lisa! I love you so much!”). He  removed Marie Yovanovitch for predicting, correctly, that Trump’s efforts to pressure Ukraine into smearing Hunter Biden would get him impeached. None of these people were angling for invitations to the Gridiron Club dinner.
  Trump also rolled back or bottled up regulations,  especially on the  environment, and tried (though mostly failed) to slash agency budgets. Many career officials  headed for the exits, especially at the Labor Department, which lost more than 10 percent of its workforce; the State, Education, and Interior departments, which lost more than 5 percent; and the Agriculture Department, which lost 5 percent. The ones who stayed kept the lights on, even as Trump tried to switch them off.
 Christi Grimm is one such hero. An official at the Health and Human Services’ Office of Inspector General (IG) since 1999, Grimm grew up in Edgewater, Colorado, where her grandfather ran the Public Works Department, providing water to the small town, building new roads and sidewalks, and maintaining city parks. “I was proud of how he made our town better,” she would later recall in  testimony before the Senate Finance Committee.
 In January 2020, Grimm was made acting inspector general at HHS. Three months later, after the pandemic hit, her office  released a survey of 323 hospitals around the country taken during the last week of March. The hospitals reported severe shortages of testing supplies, widespread shortages of N95 protective masks, and difficulty meeting the crush of hospital patients. “This is not a review of HHS response to the COVID-19 pandemic,” the report said. But of course Trump took it as a personal insult. When a reporter asked him about it at  one of his daily Covid briefings, he said, “It’s just wrong” and demanded to know the IG’s name. Later, he went on Twitter to  denounce her as partisan (never mind that Grimm had worked eight years under President George W. Bush) and called the report “Another Fake Dossier” (a reference to a discredited document by a retired British spy alleging that Russia possessed compromising information about Trump). A few weeks after that, Trump  replaced Grimm with another acting inspector general who was never confirmed by the Senate. Grimm resumed her previous role as deputy inspector general. 研究每日咖啡摄入量与全因和心血管（CV）死亡率及主要CV结局的关系。在一个接受心血管磁共振（CMR）成像的亚组参与者中，我们评估了经常喝咖啡与心脏结构和功能之间的关系。
包括招募时没有临床表现的心脏病的英国生物银行参与者。常规咖啡摄入量分为三组：零、轻度至中度（0.5-3杯/天）和高（大于3杯/天）。在多变量分析中，我们调整了主要的心血管风险因素。
我们包括468629人（56.2±8.1岁，44.2%为男性），22.1%不经常喝咖啡，58.4%每天喝0.5-3杯咖啡，19.5%每天喝&gt；每天3杯。与不喝咖啡的人相比，轻度至中度（每天0.5-3杯）喝咖啡与较低的全因死亡率（多变量HR=0.88，95%CI:0.83-0.92；p&lt；0.001）和CV死亡率（多变量HR=0.83，95%CI:0.74-0.94；p=0.006）相关，中位随访11年后发生卒中（多变量HR=0.79，95%CI:0.63-0.99 p=0.037）。CMR数据可用于30650名参与者。轻度至中度和重度咖啡摄入类别均与左室和右室舒张末期、收缩末期和卒中量的剂量依赖性增加，以及更大的左室质量有关。
每天最多喝3杯咖啡与良好的CV结果相关。经常喝咖啡也与CMR指标的可能健康模式相关，与年龄相关的心脏改变相反。

To study the association of daily coffee consumption with all-cause and cardiovascular (CV) mortality and major CV outcomes. In a subgroup of participants who underwent cardiovascular magnetic resonance (CMR) imaging, we evaluated the association between regular coffee intake and cardiac structure and function.
  UK Biobank participants without clinically manifested heart disease at the time of recruitment were included. Regular coffee intake was categorized into 3 groups: zero, light-to-moderate (0.5-3 cups/day) and high (>3 cups/day). In the multivariate analysis, we adjusted for the main CV risk factors.
  We included 468,629 individuals (56.2 ± 8.1 years, 44.2% male), 22.1% did not consume coffee on a regular basis, 58.4% had 0.5-3 cups per day and 19.5% had >3 cups per day. Compared to non-coffee drinkers, light-to-moderate (0.5-3 cups per day) coffee drinking was associated with lower risk of all-cause mortality (multivariate HR = 0.88, 95%CI : 0.83-0.92; p < 0.001) and CV mortality (multivariate HR = 0.83, 95%CI : 0.74-0.94; p = 0.006), and incident stroke (multivariate HR = 0.79, 95%CI : 0.63-0.99 p = 0.037) after a median follow-up of 11 years. CMR data were available in 30,650 participants. Both light-to-moderate and high coffee consuming categories were associated with dose-dependent increased left and right ventricular end-diastolic, end-systolic and stroke volumes, as well as greater left ventricular mass.
  Coffee consumption of up to 3 cups per day was associated with favorable CV outcomes. Regular coffee consumption was also associated with a likely healthy pattern of CMR metrics in keeping with the reverse of age-related cardiac alterations.

美国警告称，俄罗斯在未来几天内入侵乌克兰的可能性“非常明显”，可能涉及对基辅的压倒性袭击，并告诉所有剩余的美国人在未来48小时内离开乌克兰。
乔·拜登将于周六与弗拉基米尔·普京通电话。外交消息人士说，拜登在一次电话会议上告诉盟国领导人，弗拉基米尔·普京已经决定进行入侵，但国家安全顾问杰克·沙利文说：“我们没有看到任何迹象表明已经做出了最后决定，[批准]命令已经下达。”
“我要说的是，他建立和部署军队的方式，以及我们通过情报收集到的其他指标，向我们表明，俄罗斯选择军事行动的可能性非常明显，而且有理由相信这可能会在一个相当快的时间段内发生”沙利文说。
“现在，我们不能确定现在是哪一天，也不能确定时间，但我们可以说的是，即使在奥运会结束之前，俄罗斯也有可能采取军事行动。”中国冬奥会将于2月20日闭幕。
据外交消息人士透露，拜登告诉其他北约和欧盟领导人，美国认为普京已决定入侵乌克兰，这可能在未来几天内发生。
拜登向盟友发出呼吁之前，白宫举行了一次情况室会议，讨论有关俄罗斯军事集结的最新情报，以及普京的想法。
一名欧洲外交官说：“由于这一决定，实际情况将开始在地面上发生，但这并不意味着普京仍然不能退后。”他坚称，阻止这位俄罗斯领导人还为时不晚。“这条路线上还有更多的决策点。”
沙利文说：“如果你留下来，你将承担风险，但不能保证会有任何其他机会离开，而且如果俄罗斯入侵，美国军队也不可能撤离。”。
“如果俄罗斯继续对乌克兰发动攻击，很可能首先是空中轰炸和导弹袭击，这些袭击显然会导致平民死亡，而不考虑他们的国籍。随后的地面入侵将涉及大规模部队的袭击，而几乎没有任何通知。安排撤离的通信可能会被切断，并被商业化交通中断。"
周五晚些时候，俄罗斯外交部指责西方国家在媒体的帮助下散布虚假信息，以分散人们对其侵略行为的注意力。
白宫表示，拜登正在呼吁跨大西洋领导人“讨论我们对俄罗斯继续在乌克兰周围集结军事力量的共同关切，以及在外交和威慑方面的持续协调”。
一名欧洲外交官表示，美国已要求与北约盟国以及欧盟理事会和委员会负责人进行紧急通话，以分享新的情报。
西方情报机构认为，俄罗斯进攻的最可能目标是包围基辅，迫使政权更迭。
他们还认为，这种入侵的信号将是虚假的旗帜攻击，可能包括乌克兰使用化学武器的指控，这将被用作俄罗斯干预的借口。
拜登打电话几分钟后，英国外交部敦促在乌克兰的英国公民“现在通过商业手段离开，在他们仍然可用的情况下”。
据塔斯社报道，俄罗斯驻基辅大使馆表示，正在考虑让非必要人员离开。其他大使馆在周五加强了疏散。
据《瓦拉新闻》（Walla News）报道，以色列命令外交人员的家属离开该国，《基辅邮报》报道，美国已要求欧洲安全与合作组织（Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe）乌克兰监测团的美国成员在周二之前离开该国。
挪威情报局局长尼尔斯·安德烈亚斯·斯滕瑟内斯中将说，俄罗斯现在有15万军队集结在乌克兰周围，并表示是否发动袭击的决定权在弗拉基米尔·普京。
“现在，如果普京总统选择这么做的话，这首先取决于他，”斯坦斯说。“很难说这是否可能，因为这完全取决于俄罗斯总统做出的决定。”
他补充说，俄罗斯人“拥有一切所需的一切，从东部的一次小规模入侵，到乌克兰境内的一次小规模袭击，或是一次全面入侵，并可能占领乌克兰的全部或部分地区”。
西方情报机构基本上同意，普京现在已经部署了足够的军队来尝试入侵，拜登和英国首相鲍里斯·约翰逊在过去24小时发出的警告反映了这种情绪。
许多入侵场景被认为是可能的，但普遍认为，普京下令的任何军事干预都旨在实现基辅政权更迭。这可能会发生闪电袭击，旨在包围基辅，意图迫使沃洛德米尔·泽伦斯基政府垮台，并试图建立一个没有城市战争的亲俄政权。
约翰逊周四强调，任何入侵都将构成普京的大规模误判，因为乌克兰将“战斗，他们将非常坚决地抵抗”。英国部长强调，克里姆林宫认为俄罗斯的干预会受到少数人以外的任何人的欢迎，这是错误的。
在几项外交举措未能取得突破性进展后，美国提高了警惕。有报道称，周一伊曼纽尔·马克龙在莫斯科与普京进行了五个多小时的会谈，大部分时间都被这位俄罗斯领导人长篇大论的历史演讲占据，几乎没有关于时事的实质内容。
德国官员关于俄罗斯、乌克兰、德国和法国官员在柏林举行的“诺曼底式”会谈的报道一直很困难，也没有缓和紧张局势的前景。
德国总理奥拉夫·斯科尔茨定于周二访问莫斯科，会见普京，但他的发言人表示，他没有与普京采取新的外交举措。
联邦议院外交委员会主席、前部长迈克尔·罗斯在推特上说：“俄罗斯实际上在军事上吞并了白俄罗斯。所谓的“演习”实际上是对乌克兰的包围，是对波兰和波罗的海国家的具体威胁。局势仍然危险。”
英国国防部长本·华莱士在莫斯科与俄罗斯国防部长会晤后表示，他已得到保证，俄罗斯无意入侵乌克兰，并已向俄罗斯国防部长谢尔盖·绍伊古明确表示，任何入侵都将带来悲惨后果。
华莱士说，俄罗斯部署军队给了它一系列选择，包括入侵，并补充说，“重要的是不要给错误沟通或误判留出空间”。
在访问澳大利亚期间，布林肯警告说，俄罗斯入侵的影响将波及亚洲，美国及其盟友在亚洲与中国的对抗日益加剧。
布林肯在墨尔本对记者说：“如果我们允许这些原则受到挑战而不受惩罚，即使它在半个世界之外的欧洲，也会对这里产生影响——其他人正在关注。”。
“其他人正在期待我们所有人看到我们如何应对。因此，我们的团结是如此重要：我们尽一切可能，通过外交努力避免冲突，防止侵略——但同样，如果俄罗斯再次侵略，我们也要坚决。”
美国官员周五表示，五角大楼将再向波兰派遣3000名作战部队，与已经在那里集结的1700名士兵会合。
note_en:
The US has warned of the “very distinct possibility” of a Russian invasion of  Ukraine in the next few days, potentially involving an overwhelming attack on Kyiv, and told all remaining Americans to leave the country in the next 48 hours.
 Joe Biden is due to speak to  Vladimir Putin by phone on Saturday. Diplomatic sources said that Biden had told allied leaders in a call that Vladimir Putin had taken a decision to go ahead with an invasion, but Jake Sullivan, the national security adviser, said: “We have not seen anything come to us that says a final decision has been taken, [that] the go order has been given.”
  “I will say that the way that he has built up his forces and put them in place, along with the other indicators that we have collected through intelligence, makes it clear to us that there is a very distinct possibility that  Russia will choose to act militarily, and there is reason to believe that that could happen on a reasonably swift timeframe,” Sullivan said.
 “Now, we can’t pinpoint the day at this point, and we can’t pinpoint the hour but what we can say is that there is a credible prospect that a Russian military action would take place, even before the end of the Olympics.” The Winter Olympics in China close on 20 February.
 Biden has told other  Nato and EU leaders that the US believes Putin has decided to carry out an invasion of Ukraine, which could happen in the next few days, according to diplomatic sources.
 Biden’s call to allies followed a situation room meeting at the White House to discuss the latest intelligence on the Russian military buildup, and on Putin’s thinking.
 “Practical things will start to happen on the ground as a result of this decision, but that doesn’t mean that Putin couldn’t still row back,” a European diplomat said, insisting it was not too late to deter the Russian leader. “There are further decision points along the line.”
  “If you stay you are assuming risk with no guarantee that there will be any other opportunity to leave, and there is no prospect of a US military evacuation in the event of a Russian invasion,” Sullivan said.
 “If a Russian attack on Ukraine proceeds, it is likely to begin with aerial bombing and missile attacks that could obviously kill civilians without regard to their nationality. A subsequent ground invasion would involve the onslaught of a massive force with virtually no notice. Communications to arrange a departure could be severed and commercial transit halted.”
  Late on Friday the Russian foreign ministry accused western countries of spreading false information with help from the media, in order to distract attention from their own aggressive actions.
 The White House said Biden was calling transatlantic leaders “to discuss our shared concerns about Russia’s continued buildup of military forces around Ukraine, and continued coordination on both diplomacy and deterrence”.
 A European diplomat said that the US had asked for the urgent call with Nato allies and the heads of the EU council and commission, to share the new intelligence.
 Western intelligence agencies believe that the most likely goal of a Russian offensive would be to surround Kyiv and force regime change.
 They also believe that the signal for such an invasion would be a false-flag attack, possibly including allegations of Ukrainian use of chemical weapons, that would be used as a pretext for Russian intervention.
 Within minutes of Biden’s call, the UK Foreign Office urged British citizens in Ukraine “to leave now via commercial means while they remain available”.
 The Russian embassy in Kyiv said it was considering telling non-essential staff to leave, according to  Tass news agency. Other embassies stepped up evacuations on Friday.
 According to  Walla News, Israel ordered family members of diplomatic staff out of the country, and the  Kyiv Post reported that the US had called for American members of the Ukraine monitoring mission run by the Organisation for Security and Cooperation in Europe to leave the country by Tuesday.
 Vice-Admiral Nils Andreas Stensønes, the  head of the Norwegian intelligence service, said Russia now had 150,000 troops massed around Ukraine, and said the decision on whether to attack rested with Vladimir Putin.
 “Now it is first and foremost up to President Putin if he chooses to do so,” Stensønes said. “It is difficult to say whether it is probable or not probable, because it is solely up to the Russian president to make that decision.”
 He added that Russians “have all they need to carry everything out, from a minor invasion in the east to minor attacks here and there in Ukraine, or a complete invasion, with, possibly, an occupation of all or parts of Ukraine”.
 Western intelligence agencies largely agree that Putin has now put in place enough troops to attempt an invasion, a sentiment reflected in  warnings in the last 24 hours from Biden and the UK prime minister, Boris Johnson.
 A number of invasion scenarios are considered possible, but there is a prevailing belief that any military intervention ordered by Putin would be designed to achieve regime change in Kyiv. That could see a lightning attack, aimed at encircling Kyiv, with the intention of forcing the collapse of Volodymyr Zelenskiy’s government, and trying to install a pro-Russian regime without urban warfare.
  Johnson emphasised on Thursday that any invasion would amount to a massive miscalculation by Putin because Ukraine would “fight and they will resist very strongly”. Any belief in the Kremlin that a Russian intervention would be welcome by anything other than a tiny minority is mistaken, British ministers stress.
 The raised alert follows the failure of several diplomatic initiatives to lead to a breakthrough. Reports suggested that much of Emmanuel Macron’s more than five hours of talks with Putin in Moscow on Monday, was taken up by lengthy historical lectures from the Russian leader, with very little substance on current events.
 Accounts from German officials on “Normandy format” talks in Berlin between Russian, Ukrainian, German and French officials had been difficult and offered no prospects of defusing tensions.
 The German chancellor, Olaf Scholz, is due to visit Moscow to see Putin on Tuesday, but his spokesman said he was taking no new diplomatic initiatives with him.
 The head of the foreign affairs committee in the Bundestag, Michael Roth, a former minister, said on Twitter: “Russia has effectively annexed Belarus militarily. What are being called “exercises” is really the encirclement of Ukraine, and a concrete threat to Poland and the Baltic States. The situation remains dangerous.”
 After a meeting with his Russian counterpart in Moscow, the British defence secretary, Ben Wallace, said he had been assured that Russia had no intention of invading Ukraine and that he had made clear to Russia’s defence minister, Sergei Shoigu, that any invasion would have would have tragic consequences.
 Wallace that Russia’s deployment of forces gave it a range of options including an invasion, and added “it was vital not to allow room for a miscommunication or miscalculation”.
 During a visit to Australia, Blinken warned that the repercussions from a Russian invasion would spill into Asia, where the US and its allies are increasingly in confrontation with China.
 “If we allow those principles to be challenged with impunity, even if it’s half the world away in Europe, that will have an impact here as well – others are watching,” Blinken told reporters in Melbourne.
 “Others are looking to all of us to see how we respond. So that’s why it’s so important that we have this solidarity: that we do everything possible, through diplomacy, to try to avert a conflict and prevent aggression – but equally, to be resolute if Russia renews its aggression.”
 US officials said on Friday that the Pentagon would send another 3,000 combat troops to Poland to join 1,700 who already are assembling there.
title: 拜登告诉北约领导人，美国认为普京已经决定入侵乌克兰
title_en: US believes Putin has decided to invade Ukraine, Biden tells NATO leaders

当她的研究——证实了20世纪70年代的一个预测，即人类对经济增长的不可抑制的渴望将撞上一堵墙，并可能在2040年左右螺旋式地演变为文明的崩溃——去年夏天首次登上头条时，可持续发展分析师加亚·赫林顿（Gaya Herrington）有着更紧迫的担忧。
她告诉《独立报》：“这是一个非常有趣的时期，当我有一个不到两个月大的婴儿时，病毒开始传播。我在哺乳期间接受了采访。”。“在某种程度上，它从我身边经过了一点。”
这篇发表在《工业生态学杂志》（Journal of Industrial Ecology）上的学术论文为何会在现在引起轰动，不需要太多的想象力；在一个经历了数月野火、连环飓风、毁灭性洪水和热浪的世界里，对未来的黑暗愿景似乎越来越不遥远。
赫林顿女士曾担任会计跨国公司毕马威（KPMG）的可持续发展总监，现任施耐德电气（Schneider Electric）环境、社会和治理研究副总裁。施耐德电气是全球最具可持续发展能力的公司，能源和自动化领域的专家。
作为她最近在哈佛大学获得可持续发展硕士学位的一部分，她决定回顾70年代初麻省理工学院（MIT）的一项标志性研究。受欧洲智库罗马俱乐部（Club of Rome）委托的研究人员提出了一个问题：在什么情况下，人类对有限资源的消耗和猖獗的工业化会超过地球的应对能力？
通过计算机建模，麻省理工学院的研究小组用一系列变量生成了未来情景——人口、食物、工业化、不可再生自然资源的使用和污染（包括碳排放）。
1972年出版的《增长的极限》（The Limits to Growth）一书的结果令人清醒。该团队发现，所谓的“一切照旧”经济增长方式将耗尽地球的资源，并导致21世纪内的崩溃。虽然人类文明不会终结，但随着粮食生产、工业产出和人口数量的下降，人们的境况将比前几代人严重得多。
但并非所有增长极限（Limits to Growth，LtG）的未来前景都如此黯淡。麻省理工学院的团队还发现，如果社会围绕公民福祉重新安排优先事项，有效利用资源，减少污染，那么“稳定的世界”是可能的。
尽管这本书的销量达到3000万册，并以30种语言出版，但最初的反应好坏参半。一些人预见到了无休止的消费和对自然世界的破坏所带来的危险。其他人则对这项研究持严厉批评态度。
《纽约时报》的经济编辑在一篇严厉的评论中写道：“增长的极限不是对自然规律的重新发现，而是对计算机科学最古老格言的重新发现：垃圾输入，垃圾输出。”。
根据赫林顿女士50年的对比分析，以及其间几年的大量研究发现，长期目标在很大程度上经受住了时间的考验。
她的论文将LtG的四种情景与现有的最新实证数据进行了比较，以了解全球社会是否仍在走向崩溃。
总的来说，新发现与20世纪70年代的研究密切相关，并得出结论认为，未来20年，文明可能会停止经济增长，在最坏的情况下，2040年左右会出现社会崩溃。
“因此，从我的研究中可以得出的最有力的结论是，人类正走在一条限制自身增长的道路上，而不是有意识地选择自己的发展道路。然而，我的研究结果也表明，随后工农业产出的下降是否会导致人口和收入的急剧下降福利水平，”赫林顿在7月份的博客文章中写道。
我们这一代人是第一个经历气候变化的人，但我们也是最后一个能够对此采取任何行动的人。
这位分析师告诉《独立报》：“在一个有限的星球上，增长无法持续的想法是非常直观的。没有什么东西能永远增长。”。然而，她补充说，模型能够准确预测未来几十年的情况“相当罕见”。
她在谈到最初的研究时说：“这太遗憾了，因为如果我们当时注意到了这个信息，我们本来可以有一个非常平稳的过渡。”。“我现在的结论是，我们还有时间，但时间很快就用完了。我们必须做出重大改进。这将是一条崎岖不平的道路。”
她补充说：“接下来的十年将决定本世纪余下的时间。这是一个非常不寻常的时期。我们这一代人是第一个经历气候变化的人，但我们也是最后一个能够对此采取任何行动的人。”
根据她的研究，一本名为《避免崩溃的五大洞见》的书将于今年晚些时候出版。这位分析师是罗马俱乐部的顾问，也是一个工作组的成员，该工作组将在6月的联合国斯德哥尔摩+50会议上向LtG提交一份50年的最新报告。
从疫情中“重建得更好”的承诺一直是主要经济体领导人的号召，但在现实中，这种更清洁、更绿色的未来迄今尚未成形。燃烧化石燃料导致的气候危机的主要驱动因素二氧化碳和甲烷排放量继续上升。
从某种意义上说，LtG的更新提供了一个可怕的未来愿景。然而，赫林顿表示，最坏的情况并不是不可避免的，尽管窗口正在迅速关闭，以绘制一条不同的路线，并将需要进行重大改变。
例如，她说，仅仅关注净零排放目标已经过时，缺乏雄心壮志，“在税收和定价的边缘思考”也是如此。
“我们需要完全重新设想我们在世界上的角色，”她说。“因为目的是什么？让这个世界比你发现的更好一点。”。
“归根结底，我认为这就是增长的局限性。它可以比这更好，我们真的可以做得更好，感觉更好。我认为我们必须提供一个愿景的替代方案。”

note_en:
When her study – confirming a 1970s prediction that  humanity’s unquenchable desire for economic growth would hit a wall and could spiral into civilization’s collapse around 2040 – first hit headlines last summer, sustainability analyst Gaya Herrington had more pressing concerns.
 “It was a very interesting time when it went viral as I had a less than two-month-old infant. I’ve done interviews while breastfeeding,” she told  The Independent. “In a way, it passed me by a little bit.”
   It doesn’t take much of an imaginative leap to understand why this academic paper, published in the relatively obscure   Journal of Industrial Ecology, would catch fire now; when dark visions of the future seem less and less far-fetched in a world experiencing months-long  wildfires, back-to-back hurricanes, devastating floods and heatwaves.
  Ms Herrington, former sustainability director at accounting multinational KPMG, is vice president of environmental, social, and governance research at Schneider Electric, a specialist in energy and automation, which is  ranked the world’s most sustainable company.
  As part of her recent Master’s degree in sustainability at Harvard University, she decided to review an iconic study from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology ( MIT) in the early Seventies. Researchers, commissioned by European think tank the Club of Rome, had been set this question: At what point will humanity’s consumption of finite resources and rampant industrialization outstrip the planet’s ability to cope?
  Using computer modeling, the MIT group generated future scenarios with a number of variables –  population, food, industrialisation, use of nonrenewable natural resources, and pollution (which included carbon emissions).
  The results, published in 1972 as  The Limits to Growth, were sobering. The team discovered that a so-called “business-as-usual” approach to economic growth would drain the planet’s resources and lead to collapse within the 21st century. While human civilization would not end, people would be significantly worse off than previous generations amid tumbling food production, industrial output, and population numbers.
  But not all future scenarios in  Limits to Growth (LtG) were so bleak. The MIT team also discovered that a “stabilized world” was possible, if society reconfigured priorities around the wellbeing of citizens, used resources efficiently, and cut pollution.
 While the book went on to sell 30 million copies and was published in 30 languages, the initial response was mixed. Some saw a prescient warning on the dangers of endless consumption and blithe ravaging of the natural world. Others were heavily critical of the research.
  “The Limits to Growth is best summarized not as a rediscovery of the laws of nature but as a rediscovery of the oldest maxim of computer science: Garbage In, Garbage Out,”   The New York Times economics editors wrote in a scathing review.
 Five decades on Ms Herrington’s comparative analysis, along with a number of  studies in intervening years, found that  LtG  has largely stood the test of time.
  Her thesis compared four scenarios from  LtG, against the most recent empirical data available, to find out if global society was still heading towards collapse.
 Overall, the new findings aligned closely with the 1970s study and concluded that civilisation can expect a halt to economic growth in the next 20 years and, in the worst-case scenario, see societal collapse around 2040.
  “The strongest conclusion that can be drawn from my research therefore, is that humanity is on a path to having limits to growth imposed on itself rather than consciously choosing its own. However, my research results also leave open whether the subsequent declines in industrial and agricultural output will lead to sharp declines in population and welfare levels,”  Ms Herrington wrote in blog post in July.
  Our generation is the first to experience climate change but we are also the last to be able to do anything about it.
 “The idea that growth cannot continue on a finite planet is very intuitive. Nothing grows forever,” the analyst told  The Independent. However she added that it was “quite rare” for models to accurately predict decades into the future.
 “It’s such a shame because if we had heeded the message [back] then, we could have had a really smooth transition,” she said of the original research. “My conclusion now is that we still have time but we’re fast running out of it. We have to make drastic improvements. It’s going to be a bumpy road.”
 She added: “This upcoming decade is going to determine the rest of the century. It’s quite an extraordinary time. Our generation is the first to experience climate change but we are also the last to be able to do anything about it.”
  A book based on her research,  titled  Five Insights For Avoiding Collapse, will be published later this year. The analyst, who is an adviser to the Club of Rome, is also part of a working group which will present a 50-year update to  LtG at the United Nation’s Stockholm +50 meeting in June.
 A promise to “build back better” from the pandemic has been the rallying cry of leaders of major economies but in reality, this cleaner, greener future has so far failed to take shape. Carbon dioxide and methane emissions, the main drivers of the climate crisis caused by burning fossil fuels,  continue to rise.
 In one sense, the updated look at  LtG offers a terrifying vision of the future. However the worst-case scenario is not inevitable, Ms Herrington says, even though the window is rapidly closing to chart a different course, and will require dramatic change.
 For example, she says, the focus solely on net-zero emissions targets is outdated and lacks ambition, as does “thinking at the margins of taxes and pricing”.
  “We need to completely re-envision what our role is in the world,” she says. “Because what is the purpose? It’s to leave the world a little bit better than you found it.
  “Ultimately, I think that’s the  Limits to Growth message. It can be better than this, we really can do better, and feel better. I think we have to offer an alternative of what the vision could be.”
